planting.note: Plant transplants in spring after last hard frost danger passes; in zone 9 plant February–April. Supertunia Royal Velvet is a vegetatively propagated cultivar — not available as seed — producing the characteristic large, single, trailing petunias of the Supertunia brand (Proven Winners). The series was bred specifically for improved heat tolerance, self-cleaning (no deadheading required), and continuous flowering that surpasses seed-grown petunias in hot, humid conditions. In zone 9 trailing petunias perform best February–May and September–November; midsummer heat reduces bloom but the plants recover well in fall. The deep, rich purple of Royal Velvet is one of the most saturated and vivid flower colors available in the petunia category — a jewel-tone purple-violet that provides exceptional intensity in container compositions.

directions: Plant in full sun — petunias require full sun for maximum flowering; partial shade causes leggy growth and sparse bloom — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist potting mix or garden soil; feed with a balanced fertilizer every 1–2 weeks; Supertunia Royal Velvet is a trailing, vegetatively propagated petunia cultivar producing large (2–3 inch), single, deep purple-violet flowers on cascading stems that can extend 2–3 feet from the container center in hanging baskets and elevated containers — creating one of the most dramatic and voluminous container annual displays available in the spring and fall season. The Proven Winners Supertunia series was one of the breeding programs that established the modern standard for trailing, self-cleaning, high-performance petunias — combining the trailing habit, large flower size, and extended bloom without deadheading that define the contemporary container petunia market. The deep, saturated purple-violet of Royal Velvet is one of the richest and most intense colors in the petunia palette — providing a strong, vivid color anchor in mixed container compositions with white, yellow, and pink companions that would disappear if paired with a less saturated purple. In zone 9's spring and fall container garden, trailing purple petunias remain one of the most visually striking and high-impact annual container options available.
sources: Proven Winners — Supertunia Royal Velvet Petunia Grower Information
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Trailing Petunias for Florida Containers
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Spring and Fall Container Annuals for Gulf South

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–May for summer bloom or August–September for fall bloom. Cosmos bipinnatus is one of the most carefree and easy-to-grow annuals from direct seed — germinates rapidly in warm soil, grows quickly to flowering size, and self-seeds abundantly to provide volunteer plants in subsequent seasons. The Sensation series produces the classic tall (3–4 foot) cosmos with large (3–4 inch), single, eight-petaled flowers in a mix of white, pink, rose, and crimson. Avoid overly fertile soil — cosmos in rich soil produces excessive foliage with reduced flowering. Direct sow in lean, well-drained soil without pre-amendment. Self-seeding naturalizes readily in zone 9 sandy soils; allow plants to set seed for the following season's population.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Sensation Cosmos demands full sun and poor to moderately fertile, lean soil; in rich, overfertilized soil it produces lush foliage with reduced bloom; Sensation Mixed Cosmos is the classic tall-garden cosmos selection, producing the open, daisy-form, single flowers on tall, feathery-foliaged plants in a harmonious mix of white, light pink, rose-pink, and crimson — creating the airy, naturalistic, cottage-garden floral display that has made Cosmos bipinnatus one of the most beloved and widely grown annual wildflowers in American gardens. The combination of feathery, finely divided foliage and large, simple, flat-faced flowers on tall, open stems creates a transparent, light-catching quality in the garden that many gardeners describe as uniquely beautiful in its combination of substantial flower size and delicate, airy overall appearance. Cosmos is one of the premier butterfly and pollinator annuals available — the large, flat, open flowers with accessible nectar are visited by an exceptional range of beneficial insects including swallowtails, fritillaries, skippers, sulphurs, bees, and beneficial wasps. As a self-seeding species in zone 9, a single planting of Sensation Mixed provides years of successional plants without additional purchase — making it one of the most economical and lowest-effort wildflower annuals for zone 9 sunny borders and wildflower meadow plantings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cosmos bipinnatus for Florida Annual Gardens
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Cosmos bipinnatus for Southern Wildflower Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Easy Annual Wildflowers for Zone 9

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 9) for winter and spring bloom; in zone 9 fall sowing is strongly preferred over spring sowing — nigella does not transplant well (direct sow only) and performs poorly if sown when soil is warm. Cool soil and winter establishment produces the strongest plants for spring bloom. Miss Jekyll is named for garden designer Gertrude Jekyll and has been in cultivation since the early 20th century. The inflated seed pods that follow the flowers are as ornamental as the flowers themselves — striped green-and-red when fresh and parchment-brown when dry, they make excellent additions to fresh and dried arrangements. In zone 9 nigella blooms February–April and sets seed before summer heat arrives.

directions: Direct sow in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil — Miss Jekyll Nigella germinates best in cool soil (50–65°F) and does not transplant well, requiring direct sowing in place; Miss Jekyll is the standard selection of Love-in-a-Mist (Nigella damascena), producing clear sky-blue flowers — one of the most vivid and pure blues available from any cool-season annual — nestled in an extraordinary collar of finely divided, thread-like, bright green bracts that create a delicate, intricate surround for each bloom. The common name 'Love-in-a-Mist' perfectly describes the appearance: the flowers appearing to float within a misty cloud of finely divided green bracts that blur the boundary between the flower and the stem, creating an unusual, hazy, romantic floral quality distinct from any other annual. After the flowers fade, Nigella produces large, inflated, balloon-like seed pods with prominent purple-red stripes that are fully as ornamental as the flowers and provide some of the most interesting seed pod structures available from any annual garden plant — valuable both in fresh arrangements and dried for winter decorations. In zone 9 Nigella is a fall-sown, winter-growing, spring-blooming cool-season annual that provides unusual sky-blue color in the February–April period when blue-flowering options are limited.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Direct-Sow Annuals for Gulf South
University of Georgia Extension — Nigella damascena for Southern Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Love-in-a-Mist for Florida Cool-Season Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (October–November in zone 9) for winter establishment and spring bloom; larkspur requires cool soil (below 65°F) for good germination and does not transplant well. In zone 9 fall sowing is the correct approach — seeds sown in spring when soil is already warming germinate poorly and produce weak, short-lived plants that bolt quickly. The Giant Imperial series produces the largest and most densely flowered larkspur spikes in cultivation — each spike reaching 4–5 feet with densely packed, fully double flowers in colors including blue, pink, lavender, rose, and white. Plants are toxic if ingested. Self-seeds reliably in zone 9 to naturalize for subsequent seasons when plants are allowed to mature and set seed.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil with consistent cool-season moisture — larkspur germinates poorly when soil temperature exceeds 65°F; must be direct-sown in fall in zone 9 for proper spring bloom; Giant Imperial Larkspur is the premier large-flowered larkspur selection for garden and cut flower use, producing tall (4–5 foot), densely flowered spikes in the classic delphinium-like form — with fully double, spurred florets densely packed along the entire upper portion of each spike in blue, lavender, pink, rose, and white — creating one of the most visually impressive cool-season annual cut flower and garden border plants available. Annual larkspur (Consolida ajacis, formerly classified as Delphinium ajacis) is the annual equivalent of the perennial delphinium, providing the same tall spike form, clear blue and purple color range, and cottage garden character in a direct-sow annual that naturalizes readily through self-seeding in appropriate climates. In zone 9 larkspur grown from fall sowing blooms in March–May — providing one of the most impressive large-scale spring flower displays available from an annual, with the tall flower spikes creating strong vertical accents in the spring border and providing exceptional long-stemmed cut flowers. Allow plants to set seed before removing for naturalized self-seeding in subsequent years.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Larkspur for Gulf South Fall Sowing
University of Georgia Extension — Cool-Season Direct-Sow Annuals for Southern Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Consolida ajacis for Florida Winter Annual Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 9) for winter establishment and spring bloom; Phlox drummondii is native to the Gulf Coast (Texas) and is well-adapted to zone 9 conditions when fall-sown. The fall sowing strategy allows winter root establishment before the spring bloom flush. Candy Stripe produces a vivid bicolor pattern with a white or pale ground and contrasting deep pink, red, or rose-pink stripes or sectors on each petal — creating a very distinctive and eye-catching pattern not found in other phlox selections. Pinch growing tips when 4–5 inches tall to encourage branching and more flower heads. Plants self-seed reliably in zone 9, naturalizing effectively in sandy soils.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with consistent moisture during the winter growing season — Candy Stripe Annual Phlox requires full sun and cool to mild temperatures for maximum flowering, declining in summer heat; Candy Stripe Annual Phlox is a bicolor selection of Annual Phlox (Phlox drummondii), a Gulf Coast native wildflower that produces clusters of five-petaled, flat-faced flowers in a distinctive pattern of contrasting colors on each petal — the Candy Stripe pattern creating a vivid bicolor effect of deep pink or rose stripes against a lighter ground that makes this selection immediately distinctive among the more uniformly colored phlox selections. Phlox drummondii is native to the Texas Gulf Coast and is naturally adapted to zone 9 conditions — one of the few annual wildflowers that combines native origin with genuine adaptability to fall sowing and winter growing in zone 9. The clustered, domed heads of small flowers on compact, upright plants provide a full, lush bloom display from February through April in zone 9 — the combined effect of many rounded flower heads creating a colorful carpet of bloom that is extremely effective for mass planting and border edging. The strong fragrance of Annual Phlox attracts butterflies and beneficial insects in significant numbers.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Phlox drummondii Native Plant Profile
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annual Phlox for Texas Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Fall-Sown Cool-Season Annuals for Gulf South
